Experience
Hadoop Developer Full-time
Egy-Tech Solutions May 2020 – Present, Cairo, Egypt
•Project Description: The banking information had stored the data in different data warehouse systems for each
department but it became difficult for the organization to manage the data and to perform some analytics on the past
data, so it combined them into a single global repository in Hadoop for analysis.
•Analyzed the banking rates data set and created specification documents.
•Provided effort estimation for various project tasks.
•Developed Spark Scala and Spark SQL programs using Eclipse IDE in Windows/Linux environments.
•Created KPI’s test scenarios, test cases, and test result documents.
•Tested Scala programs in Linux Spark Standalone mode and set up multi-cluster on AWS to deploy Spark Scala
programs.
•Provided solutions using the Hadoop ecosystem including HDFS, MapReduce, Pig, Hive, HBase, and Zookeeper.
•Implemented solutions using large-scale server-side systems with distributed processing algorithms.
•Created reports for the BI team by using Sqoop to export data into HDFS and Hive.
•Supported and assisted in troubleshooting and optimization of MapReduce jobs and Pig Latin scripts.
•Demonstrated a deep understanding of Hadoop design principles, cluster connectivity, security, and factors affecting
system performance.
•Worked on importing and exporting data from different databases like Oracle and Teradata into HDFS and Hive
using Sqoop, TPT, and Connect Direct.
•Imported and exported data from RDBMS to HDFS/HBASE.
•Wrote client-side scripts to temporarily store data in HDFS before loading into Hive tables.
•Developed Sqoop scripts to facilitate interaction between Pig and MySQL Database.
•Involved in developing Hive Reports and partitioning Hive tables.
•Created and maintained technical documentation for launching Hadoop Clusters and for executing Hive queries and
Pig scripts.
•Ran Hadoop jobs for processing millions of records of text data.
Hadoop Developer Full-time
Vodafone Egypt January 2018 – April 2020, Giza, Egypt
•Project Description: Identified customers likely to churn and created a 360-degree customer view from various
heterogeneous data sources. Data was brought into a data lake (HDFS) and analyzed using Hadoop tools like Pig
and Hive.
•Installed and configured Apache Hadoop tools (Hive, Pig, HBase, Sqoop) for application development and unit
testing.
•Wrote MapReduce jobs to discover trends in user data usage.
•Involved in database connection using Sqoop.
•Created Hive tables, loaded data, and wrote Hive queries using HiveQL.
•Involved in partitioning a

nd joining Hive tables for query optimization.
•Experienced in SQL DB Migration to HDFS.
•Utilized NoSQL (HBase) for faster performance, maintaining de-normalized data for OLTP .
•Collected data from distributed sources into Avro models, applied transformations, standardized, and loaded into
HBase for further processing.
•Experienced in defining job flows and used Oozie to orchestrate workflows.
•Implemented Fair schedulers on the Job Tracker to share cluster resources for MapReduce jobs.
•Exported analyzed data to relational databases using Hive for visualization and BI report generation.

Connect Direct, TPT
Abdelrahman Ahmed is a Hadoop Developer with contact information including an email address, phone number, GitHub profile, LinkedIn profile, and personal website.

From May 2020 to the present, Abdelrahman Ahmed worked as a Hadoop Developer at Egy-Tech Solutions in Cairo, Egypt, focusing on consolidating banking data into a Hadoop repository for analysis. He performed tasks such as data analysis, effort estimation, program development using Spark Scala and Spark SQL, testing, and providing solutions using the Hadoop ecosystem.
The individual has experience in optimizing queries by joining Hive tables and migrating SQL databases to HDFS.
The individual utilized NoSQL (HBase) for improved performance in OLTP scenarios, maintaining de-normalized data.
Data was collected from distributed sources, transformed, standardized, and loaded into HBase using Avro models for further processing.
The individual defined job flows and used Oozie to orchestrate workflows.
Fair schedulers were implemented on the Job Tracker to share cluster resources for MapReduce jobs.
Analyzed data was exported to relational databases using Hive for visualization and BI report generation.
The individual possesses skills in Big Data Technologies including Hadoop, HDFS, MapReduce, Pig, Hive, Sqoop, Flume, Oozie, HBase, Spark, Cloudera, Storm, and Zookeeper.
The individual is proficient in programming languages such as Java, Scala, Python, Shell Scripting, SQL, and PL/SQL.
The individual has experience with databases including NoSQL, Oracle 10g, MySQL, Teradata, and RDBMS.
The individual is familiar with tools and platforms such as Linux, AWS, Eclipse IDE, Tomcat, Putty, SVN, GitHub, IBM WebSphere v8.5, SQL*PLUS, Connect Direct, and TPT.

From January 2018 to April 2020, Abdelrahman Ahmed worked as a Hadoop Developer at Vodafone Egypt in Giza, Egypt, focusing on identifying customer churn and creating a 360-degree customer view using Hadoop tools like Pig and Hive. He installed and configured Hadoop tools, wrote MapReduce jobs, and created Hive tables.

HDFS – the Hadoop Distributed File System – is a resilient and scalable data storage layer, a foundational pillar for big data endeavors. It’s not merely a repository, but a carefully architected system designed to reliably hold vast quantities of information, breaking it down into blocks and distributing them across clusters of commodity hardware. Think of it as a digital archive built for endurance and accessibility, even when individual components falter. 

It’s a frequent destination for data liberated from traditional SQL databases, offering a new home and analytical potential. Those skilled in its workings – like Hadoop Developer Abdelrahman Ahmed – understand its power lies in its integration with a wider ecosystem. Ahmed, for instance, has leveraged HDFS as the core of data consolidation for banking analysis at Egy-Tech Solutions and customer behavior modeling at Vodafone Egypt. 

He’s fluent in the languages that speak *to* HDFS – Hive for SQL-like querying, Pig for data flow scripting, and the raw power of MapReduce for custom processing. Beyond these, he’s adept at utilizing Spark, Sqoop, and other tools to move data in and out, and to unlock its insights. HDFS isn’t an island; it thrives when connected to technologies like HBase for fast access, and orchestrated by frameworks like Oozie. It’s a key component in the broader landscape of Big Data Technologies, a silent workhorse enabling analysis that would be impossible with conventional systems. Its strength isn’t just *holding* the data, but *preparing* it for discovery.

Hive is a cornerstone of the modern data landscape, a quietly powerful tool for wrangling and analyzing colossal datasets. It’s a data warehouse system built on top of Hadoop, offering an SQL-like interface to query data stored in distributed storage – primarily HDFS, but also other compatible systems. Think of it as a translator, allowing those familiar with the structured logic of SQL to navigate the complexities of Big Data. 

Hive isn’t about real-time querying; it excels at processing large volumes of data for batch processing, making it ideal for tasks like data summarization, report generation, and data mining. It’s frequently employed to transform raw data into a format suitable for Business Intelligence tools, seamlessly exporting results to relational databases for visualization and insightful reporting. 

Experienced hands, like Hadoop Developer Abdelrahman Ahmed, leverage Hive’s capabilities to unlock valuable insights. He’s utilized Hive to optimize complex queries involving joined tables, and to build the foundations for comprehensive data analysis – from consolidating banking data to constructing detailed customer profiles. Hive, alongside technologies like Spark, Pig, and MapReduce, forms a vital part of his toolkit, enabling him to build robust data pipelines and deliver actionable intelligence. It’s a key component in the broader ecosystem of Big Data technologies, a workhorse for those seeking to tame and understand the ever-growing flood of information.

MapReduce is a foundational processing engine within the broader landscape of Big Data technologies. It’s a framework for distributed processing of large datasets across clusters of computers, enabling parallel computation where data is broken down into smaller chunks, processed independently, and then recombined to yield a result. Though often overshadowed by newer tools like Spark, it remains a core component of the Hadoop ecosystem and a vital skill for data engineers. 

It’s a workhorse, frequently employed in tasks demanding significant computational power – think consolidating vast banking data for analysis, or building comprehensive customer profiles. While newer technologies offer streamlined approaches, MapReduce’s ability to handle massive volumes remains relevant. 

Individuals skilled in the Hadoop ecosystem, like Abdelrahman Ahmed, often demonstrate proficiency in MapReduce, utilizing it directly for tasks such as writing jobs to process and transform data. It’s a building block, a fundamental technique upon which more complex data pipelines are often constructed, and a testament to the enduring power of distributed computing. Fair schedulers are often implemented alongside it to ensure equitable access to cluster resources for multiple jobs running concurrently.

Eclipse IDE is Abdelrahman Ahmed’s familiar workbench, a cornerstone of his development toolkit. It’s not merely an editor; it’s the space where he crafts solutions within the vast Hadoop ecosystem at Egy-Tech Solutions. While his days are now spent wrestling with Spark Scala and Spark SQL, consolidating banking data for analysis, Eclipse remains a constant – the environment where code takes shape, where logic is refined, and where the building blocks of data pipelines are assembled. It’s a platform he’s comfortable navigating alongside Linux, Tomcat, and a suite of other tools like Putty, SVN, and GitHub, suggesting a pragmatic developer who chooses the right instrument for the task. Though the specifics of his work lean towards big data technologies like Hadoop and WebSphere, Eclipse represents the foundational layer, the familiar ground from which he launches into complex challenges. It’s a silent partner in his efforts, a testament to a well-rounded skillset honed through experience with both established and cutting-edge technologies.

Linux is the foundational bedrock upon which Abdelrahman Ahmed builds his data mastery. Not a flashy tool, but a quietly powerful presence, it’s the operating system underpinning his work with the vast Hadoop ecosystem at Egy-Tech Solutions. It’s the digital soil from which his Spark Scala and Spark SQL programs bloom, the silent partner in his data analysis and solution design for consolidating critical banking information. While he navigates a landscape of sophisticated technologies – AWS, WebSphere, even the intricacies of Connect Direct – Linux remains a constant, a reliable workhorse. It’s the environment where he wields Putty for access, interacts with repositories via SVN and GitHub, and likely leverages Eclipse IDE for development. It’s not merely a tool *used*, but the very ground on which his Hadoop expertise is cultivated, a fundamental element of his skillset as a developer shaping the future of data insights in Cairo.

HBase is a high-performance, scalable NoSQL database, favored for its ability to handle Online Transaction Processing (OLTP) workloads where speed is paramount. Unlike traditional relational databases, HBase thrives on de-normalized data – embracing redundancy for faster reads and writes. It’s a cornerstone of the broader Hadoop ecosystem, often working in concert with technologies like HDFS, MapReduce, Spark, and Zookeeper. Data, frequently sourced from diverse and distributed origins, finds its way into HBase via efficient serialization formats like Avro, ready for in-depth analysis. 

A skilled practitioner, like Hadoop Developer Abdelrahman Ahmed, leverages HBase’s strengths to build robust data solutions. He’s demonstrated proficiency in utilizing HBase for consolidating large datasets – notably banking information at Egy-Tech Solutions – and for creating comprehensive customer profiles at Vodafone Egypt. While comfortable across the Hadoop suite, including Pig and Hive, Ahmed understands HBase’s specific role in providing a fast, flexible foundation for data-driven insights. It’s a tool he employs not just for storage, but as a critical component in the entire data pipeline, from ingestion to analysis.

Abdelrahman Ahmed is a pragmatic Hadoop Developer, currently shaping data landscapes at Egy-Tech Solutions in Cairo since May 2020. He’s a builder, comfortable wrestling with large-scale banking data, consolidating it into a Hadoop repository primed for insightful analysis. He doesn’t just *use* the Hadoop ecosystem; he *solves* with it, fluent in the languages of Spark Scala and Spark SQL, and meticulous in his approach – from initial data analysis and effort estimation to rigorous testing. 

While deeply rooted in the modern Hadoop world, Abdelrahman isn’t solely a newcomer. He possesses a solid foundation in established technologies, a testament to his adaptability. He’s equally at home navigating the command line with Linux, deploying solutions on AWS, and coding within the familiar structure of Eclipse IDE. He understands the intricacies of application servers like Tomcat and IBM WebSphere v8.5, and can efficiently manage code with tools like Putty, SVN, and GitHub. His skillset extends to database interaction via SQL*PLUS, and crucially, he’s proficient with **Connect Direct**, a tool suggesting experience with established data transfer methodologies – a bridge between legacy systems and the modern data world he now inhabits. He also demonstrates familiarity with TPT, indicating a potential background in testing automation. He’s a developer who understands the full lifecycle, from conception to deployment, and the importance of connecting the old with the new.

Abdelrahman Ahmed is a Hadoop Developer, currently shaping data landscapes at Egy-Tech Solutions in Cairo since May 2020. He’s a builder, consolidating fragmented banking data into a unified Hadoop repository, transforming raw information into actionable insights. His toolkit isn’t limited to the Hadoop ecosystem itself; he’s fluent in the languages of Spark Scala and Spark SQL, crafting solutions with precision. A pragmatic problem-solver, he covers the full development lifecycle – from initial data analysis and meticulous effort estimation, through robust program development and rigorous testing. 

Beyond Hadoop, Abdelrahman demonstrates a broad technical foundation. He navigates the command line with ease in Linux, deploys and manages applications on AWS, and develops within the familiar structure of Eclipse IDE. He’s comfortable with server administration using Putty and version control through both SVN and GitHub. His experience extends to enterprise application servers like IBM WebSphere v8.5 and Tomcat, and he’s adept at database interaction via SQL*PLUS. He understands data transfer protocols like Connect Direct, and is specifically familiar with TPT – a tool that suggests a focus on testing and potentially transaction processing. He’s a developer who understands the entire pipeline, from data source to analytical output, and possesses the versatility to adapt to a diverse technological environment.

Teradata is a powerful, mature database system – a veteran in the world of data warehousing. It’s a name whispered with respect amongst those who’ve wrestled with truly massive datasets, a workhorse built for analytical processing rather than transactional speed. While newer NoSQL solutions and relational databases like Oracle and MySQL have gained prominence, Teradata remains a significant player, particularly in environments demanding scalability and complex query capabilities. It’s a system Abdelrahman Ahmed lists amongst his database experience, suggesting a familiarity with its intricacies – though his recent work focuses on the Hadoop ecosystem, consolidating banking data with Spark Scala and Spark SQL. Teradata represents a foundational element in the broader landscape of data management, a testament to enduring technology still relevant in today’s data-driven world, even as the tools around it evolve. It’s a system designed to *hold* the story, even as newer technologies are used to *tell* it.

Oozie is the quiet conductor of the Big Data orchestra. Not a performer itself, but the essential force ensuring every instrument – Hadoop, Hive, Spark, and the rest – plays in harmony. It’s a workflow scheduler, a diligent manager of complex jobs, taking defined flows and bringing them to life with precise timing and dependable execution. Those wrestling with large datasets and intricate processing pipelines rely on Oozie to orchestrate the chaos, transforming raw information into meaningful insights. It doesn’t boast flashy capabilities, but its reliability is legendary amongst those who build and maintain the data infrastructure of modern enterprises. A cornerstone skill for any Big Data specialist, Oozie is the unseen hand guiding the flow, the silent engine powering the analytics. It’s less about *what* the data is, and more about *when* and *how* it’s processed – a crucial distinction for anyone seeking to tame the wild frontier of Big Data.

RDBMS – a foundational cornerstone in the world of data management. Not flashy, but relentlessly reliable, it’s the structured heart of countless systems. Think of it as a meticulously organized filing cabinet, where information isn’t just *stored*, but categorized and linked with unwavering precision. Abdelrahman Ahmed, a Hadoop Developer with a keen understanding of diverse database technologies, acknowledges its place amongst newer approaches like NoSQL, Oracle, MySQL, and Teradata – a testament to its enduring relevance. While Ahmed now navigates the complexities of big data with Hadoop and Spark, his experience implicitly recognizes RDBMS as a fundamental building block. It’s the established order, the proven method, against which newer systems are often measured. Though not directly utilized in his current role consolidating banking data, its principles underpin the very structure he’s building *upon*. RDBMS isn’t about raw speed or flexibility; it’s about integrity, consistency, and the power of relationships – a quiet strength that continues to shape how we understand and interact with information.

Abdelrahman Ahmed is a highly capable Hadoop Developer based in Egypt, a specialist in building and maintaining robust data pipelines. He’s a pragmatic problem-solver, equally comfortable architecting solutions from the ground up or optimizing existing systems. For the past four years, since May 2020, he’s been instrumental at Egy-Tech Solutions in Cairo, consolidating critical banking data into a Hadoop repository – a task demanding precision and a deep understanding of data analysis. His toolkit here leans heavily on Spark Scala and Spark SQL, allowing him to efficiently process and prepare data for insightful analysis. 

Prior to that, Abdelrahman spent two years at Vodafone Egypt, tackling the complex challenge of customer churn. He wasn’t simply identifying *that* customers were leaving, but building a comprehensive 360-degree view of each customer using the power of Hadoop. He’s fluent in the language of big data – proficient with Pig, Hive, MapReduce, HBase, and Zookeeper – and adept at configuring and installing the necessary tools. 

Beyond core Hadoop technologies, Abdelrahman bridges the gap between the Hadoop ecosystem and traditional data sources. He expertly utilizes Sqoop to deliver reports to BI teams, and is skilled in data transfer, seamlessly importing and exporting data from Oracle and Teradata into HDFS and Hive using TPT and Connect Direct, as well as from various RDBMS systems. He’s a meticulous coder, comfortable writing both MapReduce jobs and complex Hive queries using HiveQL. You can reach him at abdelrahman.ahmed@email.com or +20 101 234 5678, and find his work showcased on his website and GitHub/LinkedIn profiles.
